name: Latvia
tagline: From the Baltic tribes and the Northern Crusades to a republic twice declared free.
summary: Latvia's history is shaped by its position on the eastern Baltic shore, a meeting point of Baltic and Finnic peoples, German crusaders, and the great powers that ringed the sea. In the thirteenth century the Northern Crusades brought German bishops and military orders who founded Riga in 1201 and forged the church-state of Terra Mariana, binding the Latvian and Livonian tribes to Latin Christendom. For centuries the land and its great Hanseatic port passed between rulers, the Livonian Order, Poland-Lithuania, Sweden, the Duchy of Courland and, from the early eighteenth century, the Russian Empire, while a Baltic German nobility dominated the countryside. A national awakening in the nineteenth century gave the Latvian people a modern sense of nationhood, and on 18 November 1918 an independent Republic of Latvia was proclaimed, its sovereignty recognised by Soviet Russia in the Treaty of Riga of 1920. That independence was extinguished in 1940 when the Soviet Union, acting under its secret pact with Nazi Germany, occupied and annexed the country, an act the Western powers never recognised; occupation, deportation, the Holocaust and decades of Soviet rule followed. Sustained by the doctrine of state continuity and by mass movements such as the Baltic Way, Latvians declared the restoration of independence in 1990, achieved it fully in 1991, and anchored the renewed state in the European Union and NATO in 2004.

founding.label: Proclamation of the Republic of Latvia
founding.detail: Modern Latvian statehood dates to 18 November 1918, when the People's Council of Latvia proclaimed an independent, democratic Republic of Latvia in Riga as the Russian and German empires collapsed at the end of the First World War. The new state had to fight a War of Independence against both Soviet and German forces before its sovereignty was secured; on 11 August 1920 Soviet Russia signed the Treaty of Riga, by which it renounced all sovereign rights over Latvia and its people. This first republic was destroyed by Soviet occupation and annexation in 1940, but Latvia's continuity as a state was never recognised as extinguished by much of the world, and independence was restored by declaration on 4 May 1990 and fully on 21 August 1991.

quickFacts.label: Capital founded
quickFacts.value: Riga founded by Bishop Albert in 1201; centre of the crusader state of Terra Mariana

quickFacts.label: Independence proclaimed
quickFacts.value: Republic of Latvia declared in Riga, 18 November 1918

quickFacts.label: Occupation and annexation
quickFacts.value: Soviet occupation June 1940 and annexation August 1940 under the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact

quickFacts.label: European Union and NATO
quickFacts.value: Joined NATO on 29 March and the European Union on 1 May 2004

eras.title: Baltic Tribes, the Northern Crusades and Terra Mariana
eras.standfirst: German crusaders and missionaries reached the eastern Baltic, founded Riga and conquered the Latvian and Livonian tribes, binding the land into the church-state of Terra Mariana.
body: Before the crusaders came, the territory of modern Latvia was settled by Baltic-speaking peoples, the Latgalians, the Couronians (Curonians), the Semigallians and the Selonians, together with the Finnic Livonians along the coast. The Latgalians were the largest and most developed of these groups, and it is from them that the later Latvian people and the country's name ultimately derive. These tribes lived in chiefdoms and small principalities along the rivers and the sea, the Daugava in particular serving as a trade artery linking the Baltic to the lands of the Rus and beyond.

In the late twelfth century German merchants from Lübeck and Bremen reached the mouth of the Daugava, and missionaries followed; the monk Meinhard arrived around 1180 and was made bishop of Üxküll. The decisive figure was Bishop Albert of Buxhoevden, who founded Riga in 1201 and, with the sanction of Pope Innocent III, established the Order of the Brothers of the Sword in 1202 to subdue and convert the pagan tribes. Over the following decades the Sword Brothers waged the Baltic phase of the Northern Crusades; after their defeat by the Lithuanians and Semigallians they were absorbed into the Teutonic Order in 1237 as its autonomous Livonian branch, the Livonian Order, and by then most of the Latvian and Livonian lands had been conquered. The conquered territory was organised as the loose confederation known as Terra Mariana, the 'Land of Mary'.

Riga prospered as the leading German town of the eastern Baltic, joining the Hanseatic League in 1282 and growing rich as an intermediary in the trade between Russia and western Europe, handling amber, timber, furs and grain. Power in Livonia was divided between the Livonian Order, the archbishop of Riga and the bishops, while a German-speaking nobility and burgher elite ruled over a Latvian-speaking peasantry, a social division that would endure for centuries. The Reformation reached the towns in the early sixteenth century, but the old order collapsed only when the Livonian War broke its fragile balance, ending the medieval church-state by 1561.

events.title: Foundation of Riga by Bishop Albert
events.summary: Bishop Albert of Buxhoevden founded Riga at the mouth of the Daugava as the base for the German mission and conquest, the city that became the centre of crusader Livonia and a great Hanseatic port.

events.title: Order of the Brothers of the Sword founded
events.summary: With papal sanction Bishop Albert established the Order of the Brothers of the Sword to subjugate and convert the Baltic tribes, launching decades of crusading warfare in Livonia.

events.title: Absorption into the Teutonic Order
events.summary: After heavy defeats the Sword Brothers were merged into the Teutonic Order as the autonomous Livonian Order; by this time the Latvian and Livonian tribal lands had largely been conquered and bound into Terra Mariana.

events.title: Riga joins the Hanseatic League
events.summary: Riga entered the Hanseatic League, the powerful association of northern European merchant towns, cementing its prosperity as the chief trading city between Russia and western Europe.

figures.name: Bishop Albert of Riga
figures.blurb: A canon of Bremen sent to Christianise Livonia, Albert founded Riga in 1201 and the Order of the Brothers of the Sword in 1202, becoming the chief organiser of the German conquest of the Latvian and Livonian lands.

figures.name: Meinhard of Üxküll
figures.blurb: An Augustinian monk who arrived on the Daugava around 1180 and was consecrated the first bishop of Üxküll, beginning the German Christian mission that the crusading orders would carry forward by force.

pullquote.text: Riga, founded in 1201, grew between the thirteenth and fifteenth centuries into one of the leading ports of the Hanseatic League on the eastern Baltic.
pullquote.attribution: On medieval Livonia and Hanseatic Riga

eras.title: Poland, Sweden, Courland and the Russian Empire
eras.standfirst: After the fall of Livonia the land was fought over by Poland-Lithuania, Sweden and Russia, while the Duchy of Courland flourished and the whole region passed at last to the Russian Empire.
body: When the Livonian Order dissolved in 1561, its lands were divided. The northern region around Riga, Livonia proper, passed under Polish-Lithuanian overlordship, while the last master of the Order, Gotthard Kettler, became the first duke of a new vassal state, the Duchy of Courland and Semigallia, in the west and south. The Baltic German nobility retained its dominance over the Latvian peasantry under each successive sovereign, and Riga remained a coveted prize. In the wars between Poland and Sweden the city was taken by King Gustavus Adolphus in 1621, and most of Livonia passed to the Swedish crown, beginning a period later remembered by Latvians as the 'good Swedish times' for its relatively orderly administration and early measures on behalf of the peasantry.

The Duchy of Courland reached a remarkable height under Duke Jacob Kettler in the mid-seventeenth century, building a merchant and naval fleet and even founding short-lived overseas colonies, on the Gambia River in West Africa and on the Caribbean island of Tobago, an extraordinary venture for so small a Baltic state. The duchy's fortunes faded after Jacob was captured during a Swedish invasion in the 1650s and his colonies were lost, but it survived as an autonomous principality for another century.

The decisive change came with the Great Northern War. In 1710 the armies of Peter the Great took Riga from Sweden, and at the Peace of Nystad in 1721 Swedish Livonia, including Riga, was ceded to the Russian Empire. The Duchy of Courland, long under Russian influence, was finally incorporated in 1795 during the third partition of Poland-Lithuania. By the end of the eighteenth century the whole of the Latvian lands lay within the Russian Empire, governed through the provinces of Livonia and Courland but still administered in practice by the privileged Baltic German elite.

events.title: Dissolution of Livonia and the Duchy of Courland
events.summary: The collapse of the Livonian Order during the Livonian War split the land: Livonia passed under Polish-Lithuanian rule, while Gotthard Kettler founded the vassal Duchy of Courland and Semigallia.

events.title: Swedish conquest of Riga
events.summary: Gustavus Adolphus of Sweden captured Riga and most of Livonia from Poland-Lithuania, opening the period of Swedish rule remembered by Latvians as the 'good Swedish times'.

events.title: Courland's overseas colonies
events.summary: Under Duke Jacob Kettler the Duchy of Courland founded short-lived colonies on the Gambia River and on Tobago, a striking commercial venture by a small Baltic state before its decline.

events.title: Russia takes Riga
events.summary: During the Great Northern War the forces of Peter the Great captured Riga from Sweden; the cession of Swedish Livonia was confirmed at the Peace of Nystad in 1721, bringing the region into the Russian Empire.

events.title: Courland annexed by Russia
events.summary: In the third partition of Poland-Lithuania the Duchy of Courland was incorporated into the Russian Empire, completing Russian rule over all the Latvian lands.

figures.name: Gotthard Kettler
figures.role: Last master of the Livonian Order, first Duke of Courland
figures.blurb: Presiding over the dissolution of the Livonian Order in 1561, Kettler secularised its western lands and became the first duke of Courland and Semigallia, founding the dynasty that ruled the duchy for two centuries.

figures.name: Jacob Kettler
figures.blurb: Under Duke Jacob the Duchy of Courland reached its commercial peak, building a fleet and founding colonies on the Gambia and Tobago, before his capture by Sweden in the 1650s broke the venture.

pullquote.text: The Swedish period in Riga and Livonia is remembered in Latvian tradition as the 'good Swedish times', before Russia took the region in the Great Northern War.
pullquote.attribution: On Latvia between the great powers

eras.title: National Awakening, Independence and the First Republic
eras.standfirst: A nineteenth-century national awakening gave the Latvian people a modern identity; out of the ruin of empire came an independent republic, recognised by treaty and then turned authoritarian under Ulmanis.
body: Within the Russian Empire the Latvians remained a peasant people subject to a Baltic German upper class, but serfdom was abolished in the Baltic provinces early in the nineteenth century, and a Latvian-speaking intelligentsia began to emerge. From the 1850s the movement of the 'Young Latvians' set out to raise the prestige of the Latvian language, collect folklore and culture, and challenge German dominance; this First Latvian National Awakening laid the foundation of a modern national consciousness. It was followed in the 1890s by the more radical, leftist New Current (Jaunā strāva), which fed into the revolutionary upheaval of 1905 and produced figures such as the national poet Rainis.

The First World War devastated the Latvian lands, much of which became a front line, and the collapse of the Russian and German empires opened the way to statehood. On 18 November 1918 the People's Council of Latvia proclaimed an independent Republic of Latvia in Riga. Independence had then to be won in a confused War of Independence fought against Soviet forces and against German and pro-German troops; victory was secured with Allied help, and on 11 August 1920 the Latvian-Soviet Treaty of Riga ended the war, with Soviet Russia renouncing all sovereign rights over Latvia 'for all future time'. A democratic constitution, the Satversme, was adopted in 1922.

The interwar republic carried out a sweeping land reform that broke up the German estates and created a class of Latvian smallholders, and it developed a lively cultural and political life. But like much of Europe it drifted toward authoritarianism: on 15 May 1934 the veteran prime minister Kārlis Ulmanis carried out a self-coup, suspending the constitution, dissolving the Saeima and the political parties, and ruling thereafter as an authoritarian leader who in 1936 also took the office of president. His regime lasted until the Soviet occupation destroyed the republic in 1940.

events.title: The First National Awakening
events.summary: The Young Latvians led a cultural revival that championed the Latvian language and folklore against Baltic German dominance, creating the basis for a modern Latvian nation.

events.title: Proclamation of the Republic of Latvia
events.summary: As the empires collapsed at the end of the First World War, the People's Council of Latvia proclaimed an independent, democratic Republic of Latvia in Riga.

events.title: Latvian-Soviet Treaty of Riga
events.summary: Concluding the War of Independence, Soviet Russia signed the Treaty of Riga, in which it voluntarily and irrevocably renounced all sovereign rights over the Latvian people and territory, recognising Latvia's independence.

events.title: Adoption of the Satversme
events.summary: Latvia adopted its democratic constitution, the Satversme, in 1922, establishing the institutions of the parliamentary first republic.

events.title: The Ulmanis coup
events.summary: Prime Minister Kārlis Ulmanis staged a self-coup, suspending the constitution, dissolving the Saeima and the parties, and establishing an authoritarian regime that lasted until 1940.

figures.name: Rainis
figures.blurb: Pen name of Jānis Pliekšāns, Rainis was the foremost Latvian poet and dramatist of the awakening, associated with the New Current; his patriotic verse became central to the modern Latvian literary and national identity.

figures.name: Kārlis Ulmanis
figures.blurb: Latvia's first head of government in 1918 and a dominant figure of the first republic, Ulmanis seized power in the coup of 1934 and ruled authoritatively until the Soviet occupation of 1940; deported to Russia, he died in captivity.

pullquote.text: By the Treaty of Riga of 1920 the Soviet government voluntarily and irrevocably renounced all sovereign rights of the Russian State over the Latvian people and Latvia's territory.

eras.title: Occupation, War and the Holocaust
eras.standfirst: Assigned to the Soviet sphere by the Nazi-Soviet pact, Latvia was occupied and annexed in 1940, its people deported and murdered under successive Soviet and Nazi regimes.
body: Latvia's fate in the Second World War was sealed in secret. The Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact of 23 August 1939 between Nazi Germany and the Soviet Union contained a secret protocol that assigned Latvia, along with Estonia and later Lithuania, to the Soviet sphere of influence. Under pressure Latvia was forced to admit Soviet military bases, and in June 1940 the Red Army occupied the country outright; after a staged election a puppet assembly requested incorporation into the Soviet Union, and Latvia was annexed in August 1940. On the night of 13-14 June 1941 the new Soviet regime carried out the first mass deportation, removing some fifteen thousand people, the political, economic and intellectual elite among them, to Siberia, where many perished.

Within days of that first deportation, Germany invaded the Soviet Union and occupied Latvia from the summer of 1941. The Nazi occupation brought the Holocaust to Latvian soil. A ghetto was established in Riga, and on 30 November and 8 December 1941 SS units under Friedrich Jeckeln shot roughly twenty-five thousand Jews, most of them from the Riga ghetto, in the Rumbula forest, one of the largest single massacres of the Holocaust before the death camps. The great majority of Latvia's prewar Jewish community was murdered during the occupation.

In 1944-1945 the Red Army reconquered Latvia, restoring Soviet rule, although a pocket in the west, the Courland Kettle, held out by German forces until the war's end. The successive occupations between 1940 and 1991, Soviet, then Nazi, then Soviet again, would become the subject of Latvia's Museum of the Occupation, founded in 1993 to document fifty-one years of foreign rule, the deportations, executions and the destruction inflicted on the Latvian state and people.

events.title: The Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact
events.summary: The Nazi-Soviet non-aggression pact and its secret protocol assigned Latvia and the other Baltic states to the Soviet sphere of influence, sealing the country's fate in the coming war.

events.title: Soviet occupation and annexation
events.summary: The Red Army occupied Latvia in June 1940; after a staged election a puppet parliament requested incorporation, and the Soviet Union annexed the country in August 1940.

events.title: The June 1941 deportations
events.summary: The Soviet regime deported some fifteen thousand people from Latvia to Siberia in a single operation, targeting the country's political, economic and intellectual elite; many never returned.

events.title: The Rumbula massacre
events.summary: Under the Nazi occupation, SS units led by Friedrich Jeckeln shot about twenty-five thousand Jews, chiefly from the Riga ghetto, in the Rumbula forest, among the largest single massacres of the Holocaust.

figures.name: Friedrich Jeckeln
figures.blurb: The senior SS and police leader who organised the Rumbula massacre of November-December 1941 and other mass killings in the occupied east; he was tried and executed in Riga in 1946.

figures.name: Paulis Lazda
figures.blurb: A Latvian-American historian whose 1993 proposal created the Museum of the Occupation of Latvia, dedicated to documenting the fifty-one years of Soviet and Nazi occupation and remembering their victims.

pullquote.text: The Museum of the Occupation of Latvia documents fifty-one years of foreign rule between 1940 and 1991, under two successive totalitarian regimes.
pullquote.attribution: Museum of the Occupation of Latvia

eras.title: Soviet Rule, Restoration and the Return to Europe
eras.standfirst: Through decades of Soviet rule and deportation Latvia's statehood survived in law and memory, until the Baltic Way and the restoration of 1990-1991 brought independence and a place in the European Union and NATO.
body: For more than four decades after 1945 Latvia was a Soviet republic, subjected to Sovietisation, Russification and large-scale immigration that altered its demography. A second great wave of repression came in March 1949, when tens of thousands of people were deported to Siberia, many of them peasants resisting the forced collectivisation of agriculture. Yet Latvia's statehood was not held to have ended: the United States, in the Welles Declaration of 1940, and many other Western governments refused to recognise the annexation of the Baltic states as lawful, sustaining the doctrine of state continuity that kept the prewar republics legally alive and allowed their diplomatic missions to function in exile throughout the Cold War.

The reforms of the late 1980s reopened the question of independence. On 23 August 1989, the fiftieth anniversary of the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact, about two million people joined hands in a human chain stretching from Tallinn through Riga to Vilnius, the Baltic Way, demanding recognition of the pact's secret protocols and the restoration of independence. On 4 May 1990 the Supreme Council of Latvia adopted the Declaration on the Restoration of Independence, reaffirming the 1918 constitution and denouncing the 1940 occupation as illegal. After the failed Moscow coup, full independence was restored by constitutional law on 21 August 1991 and swiftly won international recognition.

The restored republic anchored itself firmly in the West, joining NATO on 29 March 2004 and the European Union on 1 May 2004. The unfinished business of the Soviet era was the eastern district of Abrene, which had been part of interwar Latvia under the 1920 treaty but was transferred to the Russian SFSR in 1944 and never returned. Latvia long linked any border settlement to the 1920 treaty, but in order to conclude relations with Russia it dropped that claim: the 2007 Latvia-Russia border treaty fixed the frontier and renounced the Abrene claim, even as Latvia's Constitutional Court reaffirmed the principle of state continuity. With that, the modern Latvian state settled its borders and completed its return to Europe.

events.title: The March 1949 deportations
events.summary: In Operation Priboi the Soviet authorities deported tens of thousands of Latvians to Siberia, many of them farmers resisting collectivisation, in the largest single act of repression of the postwar years.

events.title: The Baltic Way
events.summary: On the fiftieth anniversary of the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact, about two million people formed a human chain from Tallinn through Riga to Vilnius, demanding the restoration of Baltic independence.

events.title: Declaration on the Restoration of Independence
events.summary: The Supreme Council of Latvia adopted the declaration restoring independence, reaffirming the 1918 constitution and declaring the 1940 Soviet occupation illegal.

events.title: Full restoration of independence
events.summary: After the collapse of the Moscow coup, the Supreme Council adopted a constitutional law fully restoring the independence of the Republic of Latvia, soon recognised internationally.

events.title: Accession to NATO and the European Union
events.summary: Latvia became a member of NATO on 29 March 2004 and of the European Union on 1 May 2004, anchoring the restored state firmly within Western institutions.

events.title: Latvia-Russia border treaty
events.summary: The border treaty with Russia entered into force, fixing the frontier and renouncing Latvia's claim to the Abrene district transferred to the Russian SFSR in 1944, while the Constitutional Court reaffirmed state continuity.

figures.name: Vaira Vīķe-Freiberga
figures.blurb: A scholar who returned from exile to lead the restored republic, Vīķe-Freiberga served as president from 1999 to 2007 and was a leading advocate of Latvia's accession to the European Union and NATO in 2004.

figures.name: Anatolijs Gorbunovs
figures.blurb: As chairman of the Supreme Council he presided over the adoption of the 4 May 1990 Declaration on the Restoration of Independence, a central figure in Latvia's peaceful transition from Soviet rule.

pullquote.text: On 4 May 1990 the Supreme Council reaffirmed the 1918 constitution and denounced the Soviet occupation of 1940 as illegal, beginning the restoration of independence.
pullquote.attribution: On the renewal of Latvian statehood

figures.name: Bishop Albert of Riga
figures.blurb: The crusading bishop who founded Riga in 1201 and the Order of the Brothers of the Sword in 1202, the chief architect of the German conquest and Christianisation of the Latvian and Livonian lands.

figures.name: Kārlis Ulmanis
figures.blurb: Latvia's first head of government in 1918 and the dominant politician of the first republic, who seized power in the 1934 coup and ruled until the Soviet occupation of 1940; he died in Soviet captivity.

figures.name: Rainis
figures.blurb: The foremost poet and dramatist of the Latvian awakening, associated with the New Current, whose work became a cornerstone of modern Latvian national and literary identity.

figures.name: Vaira Vīķe-Freiberga
figures.blurb: President of Latvia from 1999 to 2007 and a prominent international voice for the Baltic states, who guided the country into the European Union and NATO in 2004.
